evidence_quotes.quote: Nowadays, a new kind of video game has become common especially those that require an internet connection to access servers fully controlled by the developer or publisher.
they were led to believe they were buying full ownership of the game, when in reality they were only renting a license.

paragraphs.text: Hello, Ive read this campaign carefully, and while I agree with the points it raises such as dark patterns, addictive design, and similar concerns I believe theres another issue that could be included in this initiative, one that increasingly affects consumers of digital products. Ive been playing video games since I was a child, back when my family would gift me the latest console and a few games. To this day, I can still plug in those consoles, insert the game I want to play, and enjoy them just as I did back then. Nowadays, a new kind of video game has become common especially those that require an internet connection to access servers fully controlled by the developer or publisher. In many cases, most or even all of the games functionality relies on these servers. This development model doesn't necessarily pose a problem for the consumer unless the company decides to shut those servers down, leaving players who paid for the game without access to it. This is exactly what happened with The Crew, a video game developed by Ubisoft, which is now the subject of a lawsuit by users who suddenly lost all access to the game they purchased. In the lawsuit, players claim they were led to believe they were buying full ownership of the game, when in reality they were only renting a license. The list of video games that people have purchased and can no longer play continues to grow as this model becomes more widespread. The campaign Stop Killing Games maintains a list of dead games, where the community has been adding titles that are no longer playable or are at risk of becoming so. As of now, 358 games have been reported as dead and 389 more are at risk, with no end-of-life plans disclosed by the developers. In my opinion, the fact that I can pay full price for a digital product and then be unable to access it a few years or even months later, just because the developer or publisher decides to stop supporting it for whatever reason they see fit, is a complete violation of my rights as a consumer. Thats why Ive signed campaigns like Stop Destroying Videogames, a European Citizens' Initiative that has gathered over 1,440,000 signatures. Its goal is to require developers to provide a proper end-of-life plan for online games, so that when they choose to stop supporting a title, they offer alternatives that allow players to keep enjoying the game in a way that stays true to the original experience. And for the same reason, I would like to see the Digital Fairness Act include regulations for video game development, to ensure that future games protect consumer rights and put an end to this ongoing destruction of digital content. Thanks for your time
